food_name: Kuih Dadar (Pandan Crepe Rolls with Sweetened Grated Coconut Filling)
aliases: 班丹椰丝卷 (Bān Dān Yē Sī Juǎn)

chinese: 班丹椰丝卷 (Bān Dān Yē Sī Juǎn)

food_subgroup: Traditional kuih — thin pandan-flavoured crepe rolled with sweetened grated coconut (inti) filling cooked with gula melaka (palm sugar)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for Kuih Dadar or Malay/Peranakan kuih preparations. No defensible exact or close-parent URI identified without fabrication. Closest conceptual parent would be 'filled crepe food product' but no confirmed FoodOn URI exists for this.
ontology.foodex2_description: Pancakes and crepes — FoodEx2 closest parent category for thin batter-based cooked crepe products. Used as closest_parent match; no specific Malay kuih code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Kuih Dadar. A0CKP (Pancakes and crepes) is used as the closest defensible parent. The coconut filling component is not captured by this code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept identified for Kuih Dadar or equivalent Malay/Peranakan kuih. SNOMED CT food concepts do not extend to traditional Southeast Asian kuih preparations.

allergens: gluten (if wheat flour used in crepe)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is 2 rolls weighing approximately 90g, as commonly sold at Malay kuih stalls and Peranakan dessert counters in Singapore and Malaysia.

nutrient_source.title: Kuih Dadar — Health Promotion Board Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo) for Kuih Dadar. This is an approved primary regional source for Singaporean and Malaysian dishes. Values are reported per 100g edible portion. Minor micronutrients (copper, manganese, vitamin K) supplemented from USDA FoodData Central analogous coconut-based dessert components where HPB data was not available, as documented in notes.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Composite Crepe and Coconut-Palm Sugar Foods — Analogous Estimate Based on University of Sydney GI Database Reference Foods
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Kuih Dadar exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI ~65 (medium) is estimated as a composite: thin wheat/rice flour crepe (GI ~65, analogous to plain pancake/crepe entries in the Sydney GI Database) combined with grated coconut filling sweetened with gula melaka (palm sugar GI ~35). The fat and fibre content from fresh grated coconut moderates the overall glycaemic response. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (65 × 30.6g carbohydrate in 90g serving) / 100 ≈ 19.9. This is a defensible analogous estimate; direct testing of Kuih Dadar has not been published.

hypertension.guidance: Kuih Dadar contributes approximately 108mg sodium per standard 2-roll serving, which is well within the recommended limit of 2000mg/day for hypertensive individuals, making it a low sodium concern when consumed in a single serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium GI (~65) and moderate glycemic load (~19.9 per 2-roll serving) with high sugar content from gula melaka coconut filling (18g sugar/100g; ~16g per 2 rolls)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit Kuih Dadar to one standard serving (2 rolls, ~90g) per occasion; the gula melaka filling provides ~16g of sugar per serving and a glycemic load of ~19.9, which is meaningful for blood glucose management, and the perceived 'lightness' of the thin pandan crepe can lead to overconsumption.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat content (8g/100g; ~7.2g per 2-roll serving) predominantly from fresh grated coconut (lauric acid / MCTs)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Kuih Dadar provides approximately 7.2g of saturated fat per 2-roll serving, primarily as medium-chain triglycerides (lauric acid) from fresh grated coconut, which is less atherogenic than long-chain animal saturated fat but still represents 36% of the recommended daily saturated fat limit (20g/day) and should be consumed in moderation by individuals with cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kuih Dadar (also known as Kuih Ketayap or Dadar Gulung) is a classic Malay and Peranakan traditional kuih — a thin, soft, emerald-green pandan-scented crepe rolled around a fragrant filling of freshly grated coconut cooked with gula melaka (palm sugar) and a pinch of salt. The crepe batter is made from plain or rice flour, coconut milk, egg, and fresh pandan juice, which gives it its distinctive vivid green colour and floral aroma. The filling (inti) is prepared by sautéing freshly grated coconut with gula melaka until the sugar caramelises and coats the coconut, producing a dark, moist, and intensely sweet-savory centre. The result is a delightful textural contrast between the delicate, slightly chewy crepe exterior and the rich, juicy coconut filling. Kuih Dadar is a staple tea-time snack sold at Malay kuih stalls, Peranakan restaurants, and pasar malam (night markets) across Singapore, Malaysia, and Indonesia. Nutritionally, a 2-roll serving (~90g) provides approximately 220 kcal, 3.6g protein, 9g fat (7.2g saturated, predominantly coconut MCTs), 30.6g carbohydrate, and 16g sugar. The medium GI (~65) and glycemic load (~19.9) make it a moderate glycaemic concern for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The high saturated fat from fresh coconut (7.2g per serving) warrants moderation for cardiovascular health. It is vegetarian, halal, and dairy-free.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo), which is the approved primary regional source for Singaporean and Malaysian dishes. The food is an exact match in the HPB database. Minor micronutrients (copper, manganese, vitamin K) were supplemented from USDA FoodData Central analogous components where HPB data was unavailable. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database reference foods, which is the approved GI source.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Kuih Dadar is not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of April 2026. 2. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for this specific preparation is not reported in the HPB Singapore database. 3. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero per HPB Singapore source — no hydrogenated fats used in traditional preparation). 4. Copper (0.08mg/100g), manganese (0.12mg/100g), and vitamin K (1.0mcg/100g) values were supplemented from USDA FoodData Central analogous coconut-based dessert components (confidence 0.9) as these micronutrients were not individually reported in the HPB Foodo entry. 5. GI of ~65 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database reference foods for plain crepe/pancake (GI ~65) and gula melaka palm sugar (GI ~35); no direct GI measurement for Kuih Dadar has been published. 6. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (65 × 30.6g available carbohydrate in 90g serving) / 100 ≈ 19.9. 7. FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Pancakes and crepes) used as closest defensible parent; no specific Malay kuih code exists. 8. All core macros (protein_g 4.0g, fat_g 10.0g, carbohydrate_g 34.0g) are non-zero and nutritionally consistent with a coconut-filled pandan crepe preparation. 9.
